mainText:
**Ohio in a Handbasket**
By Jonny Gartner
May 9, 2026

The year I turned 21, I spent the summer living with my grandmother in her sleepy southwestern Ohio farming town. Each afternoon, I would stroll down her driveway to collect the mail. Grandma and I would sort the important envelopes from the junk, placing the latter into a Longaberger basket that sat by the stove.

Standing ten inches tall and about six inches wide, this Longaberger basket was one of many throughout my grandmother’s house, evoking nostalgia from my childhood. The Longaberger Basket Company was a titan of Midwestern manufacturing and a cultural icon during the rise of multilevel marketing in the late 20th century. My grandmother’s house was just as full of Tupperware as it was of Longaberger baskets.

Founded in 1972 in Dresden, Ohio, the company drew on nearly a century of basket-making expertise from the Longaberger family. It began with just five weavers in a single building. Over the next 25 years, it evolved into a "basket empire," achieving over $1 billion in sales by 2000 and employing more than 8,000 direct employees and 70,000 sales associates nationwide. The company’s stunning rise was symbolized by its 180,000-square-foot basket-shaped headquarters, now vacant on the outskirts of Newark, Ohio.

This vacancy followed the company’s closure in 2016 and its bankruptcy filing in 2018. The building stands untouched, surrounded by cornfields and soybeans, its large, heated basket handles designed to prevent icing.

Ohio is littered with abandoned manufacturing sites, serving as a testament to the rapidly changing economic landscape in America. The nostalgia surrounding these sites has prevented their demolition while fostering hopes for future development. They symbolize an agricultural and manufacturing state transitioning into a new era.

After my grandmother passed last summer, I inherited that small mail basket from her kitchen counter. Today, it sits on my desk, filled with my own mail, a reminder of the enduring significance of everyday artifacts in our nation’s history.
